History of Present Illness: Ms. [**Known lastname 62201**] is a 67 year-old woman with Diabetes mellitus, end stage renal disease on hemodialysis, morbid obesity, coronary artery disease, and known L4-5 disc herniation who presented with right leg weakness, increased back pain, and worsening disc protrusion at L3-4.
Immediately after a fall at [**Hospital **] rehab one day prior to admission here, patient experienced increased back pain with radiation down her leg and more difficulty moving her right leg.
. Evaluation at [**Hospital6 2561**] included an MRI which showed worsening L3-4 disc protrusion.
She also demonstrated some myoclonus/asterixis on exam and received some iv ativan (amount unclear).
. Exam in the ED here notable for decreased rectal tone and decreased sensation in the medial thigh.
She had leg weakness more prominent on the right side, some decreased sensation on right leg in an L3-4 distribution, and absent DTRs in the RLE.
MRI revealed 1)Epidural hematoma extending from L2-L3 level to L4-L5 level resulting in marked mass effect on the thecal sac and as a result, compression of the nerve roots.
2) Moderate disc bulge at L4-L5 resulting in moderate stenosis of the spinal canal at that level.
3) Small disc herniation at L5-S1 level does not appear to cause significant spinal stenosis.
There are bilateral facet degenerative changes at these levels which resulted in severe left sided neural foraminal narrowing.
Pt underwent emergent L3 laminectomy/discectomy [**4-12**] given concern for cauda equina syndrome.
After dialysis removing 2L followed by successful extubation to CPAP with weaning to nasal cannula oxygen supplementation, pt was transferred to the medicine service in stable condition.

TITLE:    OROPHARYNGEAL VIDEOFLUOROSCOPIC SWALLOWING EVALUATION    EVALUATION:    An oral and pharyngeal swallowing videofluoroscopy was performed today    in collaboration with Radiology.  Nectar-thick liquid (1 tsp), Honey    thick liquid (1 tsp), and pureed consistency barium (2 tsps) were    administered.
Results follow:    ORAL PHASE:    Bolus formation and bolus control were both moderate-severely reduced    and c/b prespill of nectar into the pharynx.
Chewable solids were then    deferred given concern for reduced mastication/bolus formation.
A-P    tongue movement was also moderately reduced and c/b  tongue pumping and    weakness (puree>nectar).
Additionally, mild-moderate oral cavity    residue was observed with all trialed consistencies.
PHARYNGEAL PHASE:    Swallow initiation was moderately delayed, laryngeal elevation was    moderately reduced, and epiglottic deflection was moderate-severely    reduced.
Laryngeal valve closure was also mildly reduced at the height    of the swallow and bolus propulsion was moderately reduced.
These    deficits resulted in moderate-severe residue was noted within the    valleculae and pyriform (valleculae>pyriform).
However, velar elevation    and pharyngeal transit time were WNL for individual swallows.

ANTERIOR TO POSTERIOR POSITION: DNT    ASPIRATION/PENETRATION:    Moderate aspiration of puree, honey thick liquid, and nectar thick    liquid was observed after the swallow [**3-21**] overflow of pharyngeal    residue/penetration during the swallow, which the pt was unable to    successfully strip.
Additionally, aspiration of nectar was also noted    before the swallow [**3-21**] prespill.
Pt was not sensate to all episodes of    aspiration and cued coughing was ineffective at clearing.
TREATMENT TECHNIQUES:    Repeat swallows mildly effective at reducing oral and pharyngeal    residue, but did not prevent aspiration.

SUMMARY:    Pt presents with a severe dysphagia with moderate aspiration of all    trialed consistencies, including pudding.  Aspiration likely resulted    from reduced laryngeal elevation, swallow initiation, and pharyngeal    weakness which resulted in a overflow of pharyngeal residue into the    laryngeal vestibule.
It is thus recommended that the pt remain NPO    with no oral meds or ice chips and alternative means for primary    nutrition.
Given pt's significant dysphagia, it is likely that recovery will be    prolonged and more long term alternative means of nutrition should be    considered during the recovery process.
Please consult with Nutrition    to assist in decisions regarding alternative means of nutrition.

Recommend swallow therapy at acute rehab to complete repeat video    swallow evaluation, begin therapeutic PO trials as able, and to    consider swallow exercises including [**Last Name (un) 10569**] Head Lifts, [**Female First Name (un) 10570**]    Maneuver, and isometric laryngeal valve closure exercises.
If pt    remains at this facility we will f/u as able to begin swallow therapy,    but pt is likely to have more success with therapy techniques as he    improves and is safe to leave the ICU.

RECOMMENDATIONS:    1.) NPO including no oral meds or ice chips    2.) Alternative means for all nutrition/medication    3.) Q4 oral care    4.) Please consult with Nutrition to assist in decisions regarding    potentially longer term alternative means    5.) Acute rehab with video swallow f/u with SLP and swallow exercises    6.) We will f/u as able to begin swallowing exercises    7.) Please page if we can be of further assistance    These recommendations were shared with the patient, the nurse and the    medical team.

The patient was initially admitted to CT Surgery Service, however was transferred after admission to the Medicine Service given multiple medical problems, including E. coli urinary tract infection, pneumonia, congestive heart failure, acute renal failure, onset since the time of admission.

The Renal Service was also consulted given the patient's acute renal failure, and it was decided that his coronary artery bypass grafting should be delayed until resolution of his acute renal dysfunction.

He was followed by Medicine and Renal Services for approximately ten days, until on [**12-30**], he was brought to the Operating Room at which time he underwent coronary artery bypass grafting times three.  Please see the operative report for full details.

In summary he had a coronary artery bypass grafting times three with a LIMA to the left anterior descending, saphenous vein graft to the posterior descending artery, and saphenous vein graft to obtuse marginal.
Additionally, the patient had an intra-aortic balloon pump placed during the surgery.
He tolerated the operation and was transferred from the Operating Room to the Cardiothoracic Intensive Care Unit.
At the time of transfer, the patient had Milrinone 0.5 mcg/kg/min, Lidocaine 2 mg/min, Neo-Synephrine to maintain a mean arterial pressure of greater than 60, and Propofol for sedation.
The patient did well in the immediate postoperative period; however, he remained intubated and sedated on the day of his surgery.
On postoperative day #1, the patient continued to be hemodynamically stable.
Additionally, the patient was weaned from his Neo-Synephrine and his Lidocaine infusion.

His Milrinone drip was weaned to 0.125 mg, and he tolerated this well.  Following that, his intra-aortic balloon pump was weaned to 1:2, and on postoperative day #2, his intra-aortic balloon pump was discontinued.
On postoperative day #2, the patient's balloon was weaned to 1:3, which was not tolerated hemodynamically.
His intra-aortic balloon pump was returned to 1:1, and there was no further attempts at weaning on postoperative day #2.
On postoperative day #3, the patient's balloon was successfully weaned and ultimately removed.
Additionally the patient was noted to have atrial fibrillation with a ventricular response between 90 and 120, at times becoming hypotensive with a faster heart rate.
The patient was restarted on the Neo-Synephrine drip at that time.
Finally on postoperative day #3, the patient's mediastinal chest tubes were also removed.

Following discontinuation of the Milrinone, the patient remained hemodynamically stable throughout the next 24 hours, and on postoperative day #6, he was transferred to the floor for continuing postoperative care and cardiac rehabilitation.
Once on the floor, the patient was noted to have a 20+ beat-run of ventricular tachycardia.
The Electrophysiological Service was consulted, and on postoperative day #8, the patient was brought to the Electrophysiology Lab for EP testing.
He was found to have inducible monomorphic ventricular tachycardia, and a dual-chamber ICD was implanted at that time.
Following implantation of the ICD, the patient was returned to the floor for continuing postoperative care.

Over the next several days, the patient's creatinine again trended upward to a high of 3.5.  The Renal Service was reconsulted, and from that point forward, the patient was followed by the Renal Service, as well as Electrophysiology and Cardiothoracic Surgery.
Over the next several days, the patient had an uneventful postoperative course.
Following discontinuation of all nephrotoxic medications, including Lasix, the patient's creatinine trended slowly trended down.
On postoperative day #12, it was decided that the patient was stable and ready to be discharged home.
